title: Glued Together Sounds
learningGoal: The learner will master consonant-to-vowel phonological linking across phrasal verb boundaries (e.g., 'Turn on' as /tɜːrn ɒn/ -> /tɜːrnɒn/), mimicking native continuous speech flow.
instructions: In English, when a word ends in a hard letter (like N or P) and the next word starts with a soft vowel letter (like O or U), native speakers squish them together into ONE SOUND! Listen and copy this 'linking' sound.

contentItems.text: Native speakers do not say 'Wake... Up'. They say 'Way-CUP'. Let's practice!

targetPhrasesOrWords.phoneticHint: Take the 'K' from Wake and glue it to the 'U' in up. 'Way-CUP'.
targetPhrasesOrWords.commonMispronunciation: Pausing between the two words (Wake ... up).

targetPhrasesOrWords.phoneticHint: Take the 'T' and push it into the U. 'Geh-TUP'. (In American English, it sounds like 'Geh-DUP').

targetPhrasesOrWords.commonMispronunciation: Saying 'Turrnn... On' with a heavy pause.

targetPhrasesOrWords.phoneticHint: Take the 'T' and push it into the O. 'Puh-TON'.
Take the 'K' and push it into the O. 'Tay-KOFF'.
This is different! Two hard consonants. The 'T' disappears! It sounds like 'Sihhh-down'. Do not pop the T.
targetPhrasesOrWords.commonMispronunciation: Popping a very loud T sound (Sit-t-t-t down).

targetPhrasesOrWords.phoneticHint: Take the 'D' and push it into the 'U'. 'Stan-DUP'.
Glue the K to the A. 'Loo-KAT'.
Glue the V into the U. 'Gih-VUP'.
Glue the M to the O. 'Cuh-MOUT'.
Glue the D to the O. 'Hole-DON'.
Smooth glide between the two 'O' vowels. 'Gow-won'.
Glue the N into the U. 'Clee-NUP'.

expectedPronunciationText: Wake up Turn on Sit down Put on Clean up

feedbackRules.onPass: Awesome! You are speaking exactly like a native! You successfully 'linked' the consonant sounds directly into the vowels, making the phrasal verbs sound like one smooth word.
feedbackRules.onFail: Keep practicing! You are pausing too much between the words. Look at the syllable splits. Say it like 'Tur-NON', not 'Turn...(pause)...On'. Let the sound flow continuously!
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Watch out for 'Sit down'. Because T and D are both hard consonant sounds made with your tongue, native speakers drop the T entirely so it flows!

adminCorrectAnswerSet.exactCorrectTranscript: wake up get up turn on turn off put on take off pick up sit down stand up look at give up come out hold on go on clean up
acceptedVariants: wake up get up turn on turn off put on take off pick up sit down stand up look at give up come out hold on go on clean up
adminCorrectAnswerSet.phoneticApproximation: Forces consonant-to-vowel catenation across the verb-particle boundary, completely eliminating the glottal stop separation typical of L1 interference.

hintText: Read the 'Phonetic Hint' for each word. Try saying 'Clee-NUP' repeatedly until it feels natural.
commonMistakes: Inserting a glottal stop (a tiny pause) between 'Turn' and 'On'.
